value.prior_session_context: June 26 followed a blockbuster Thursday session (June 25) in which the Nikkei 225 surged +4.61% to 72,366.34, the KOSPI jumped +5.42% to 8,930.30, and the KOSPI and Nikkei had both hit record highs earlier in the week on the Micron earnings catalyst. The Friday session reversed those gains sharply, as profit-taking and renewed tech/AI valuation concerns drove heavy losses across Japan, South Korea, and mainland China. Hong Kong extended its losing streak. Australia was the sole regional gainer.
value.week_context: MSCI's index of Asian stocks outside Japan fell nearly 3% on Friday (Reuters). The KOSPI was set for a roughly 5% weekly decline; the Nikkei fell ~4% on the week after hitting an all-time high of 73,007 earlier in the week.

market_session: Asia regular cash-equity sessions, Friday 2026-06-26 (all closed)
text: Friday, June 26, 2026 was a regular trading day for all referenced Asian cash-equity venues (Tokyo, Hong Kong, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Seoul, ASX), with no market holidays. The session was the mirror image of Thursday: profit-taking after record-high runs earlier in the week, renewed AI/chip valuation concerns after Apple's price hikes and Nasdaq's extended losing streak, and a broad risk-off rotation hit Japan, South Korea, and mainland China hard. Hong Kong extended losses. Australia eked out a small gain on rising gold and copper prices. AP described the session as 'shares plunged Friday in Asia, led by heavy losses in Japan and South Korea as traders sold to lock in gains from recent rallies in stocks related to artificial intelligence.'

nikkei_225.basis: TE news stream close headline: 'The Nikkei 225 Index dropped 3271 points or 4.52 percent on Friday to close at 69,095 points.' AP independently reported 'shed 4.4%.' TE CFD stats table: 69,175 / -4.41%. Yahoo Finance: 69,360.88 / -4.15% (day's range 68,639.84-71,786.28). Previous close 72,366.34 (Jun/25, multi-source confirmed). Cash close used as durable anchor; CFD basis disclosed.

kospi.basis: Trading Economics KOSPI dated table Jun/26: 8,411.21 / -519.09 / -5.81%. Previous close 8,930.30 (Jun/25). AP reported 'plunged 7.7%' (intraday extreme; close tightened to -5.81%). Reuters: 'lost as much as 5.8%.'

hang_seng.basis: Trading Economics HK50 dated table Jun/26: 22,671.86 / -405.05 / -1.76%. AP reported 'lost 1.9%.' TE news stream: 'fell 1.8%, or 405 points, to close at 22,672.' Previous close 23,076.91 (Jun/25). Yahoo Finance: 22,671.86 / -1.76%.

shanghai_composite.basis: Xinhua: 'Shanghai Composite Index down 2.26 percent to 4,027.26 points'; Trading Economics SHANGHAI table Jun/26: 4,027.26 / -2.26%. Previous close 4,120.28 (Jun/25).

asx_200.basis: Yahoo Finance: 8,764.20 / +0.18%. TE news stream: 'edged up 16 points, or 0.2%, to close at 8,764.' TE CFD stats table: 8,765.80 / +0.20%. Previous close 8,749.90 (Jun/25). Week: -0.7%. Sole regional gainer.

calculation.method: Nikkei 225 close 69,095 vs prior 72,366.34; KOSPI 8,411.21 vs prior 8,930.30; Hang Seng 22,671.86 vs prior 23,076.91; Shanghai Composite 4,027.26 vs prior 4,120.28 (Xinhua close); ASX 200 8,764.20 vs prior 8,749.90.

text: Asian equity indexes closed sharply lower on Friday, June 26, 2026, reversing Thursday's chip-led rally. Japan's Nikkei 225 dropped 3,271 points (-4.52%) to 69,095 and South Korea's KOSPI plunged 519.09 points (-5.81%) to 8,411.21, as profit-taking and renewed AI/chip valuation concerns drove heavy selling. Mainland China's Shanghai Composite fell 2.26% to 4,027.26. Hong Kong's Hang Seng lost 405.05 points (-1.76%) to 22,671.86, extending its decline. Australia's ASX 200 was the sole regional gainer, edging up 14.30 points (+0.16%) to 8,764.20 as rising gold and copper prices supported miners.

softbank_group.basis: TE Japan news stream close headline: 'Losses were led by Softbank (-12.19%)'

taiyo_yuden.basis: TE Japan news stream close headline: 'Taiyo Yuden (-11.48%)'

kioxia_holdings.basis: TE Japan news stream intraday: 'Kioxia Holdings (-4.1%)'

samsung_electronics.basis: TE KOSPI news stream: 'Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix falling 6.28% and 8.95%'

smic.basis: TE HK50 news stream: 'Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation (-6.8%)'

value.catalyst: Profit-taking after both the Nikkei and KOSPI hit record highs earlier in the week. Apple's price hikes on iPads and Macs (announced June 25) fueled structural inflation worries from massive AI spending and limited component availability. The Nasdaq extended its losing streak overnight. US core PCE reached 3.4% (highest since October 2023), reinforcing expectations the Fed could keep rates higher for longer.
value.interpretation: The chip/memory complex that drove Thursday's historic rally reversed violently. The KOSPI's -5.81% and Nikkei's -4.52% were directly attributed to AI/chip profit-taking. The reversal underscores the volatility that AP described as 'typical of recent volatility as investors react to developments in the AI sector.' Both indexes had hit all-time highs earlier in the week (Nikkei 73,007; KOSPI 9,385.59).

text: Asian chip and tech stocks reversed violently on Friday, undoing much of Thursday's Micron-led rally. In Tokyo, SoftBank Group closed -12.19%, Taiyo Yuden -11.48%, Advantest -10.67%, and Tokyo Electron -4.26%. In Seoul, Samsung Electronics fell -6.28%, SK Hynix -8.95%, and SK Square -9.43%. Hong Kong's SMIC dropped -6.8%. The catalyst was multi-layered: profit-taking after record-high runs earlier in the week, Apple's price hikes fueling structural inflation concerns from AI-driven memory costs, and the Nasdaq's extended losing streak. The KOSPI's -5.81% and the Nikkei's -4.52% were the worst daily performances in the current cycle, and underscore the two-way volatility that has defined the AI trade.

hang_seng.basis: TE HK50 table Jun/26; Yahoo Finance historical; TE news stream: 'fell 1.8%, or 405 points, to close at 22,672'

context.ipo: Lingyi iTech (Apple supplier) debuted in Hong Kong with HK$8.3 billion IPO — the largest in Asia this year — but failed to improve broader market sentiment.
context.loss_streak: Hong Kong extended its decline from the prior session (June 25 Hang Seng at 23,076.91, lowest since May 2025). The June 26 close of 22,671.86 deepened the pullback.

losers.basis: TE HK50 component table Jun/26 and news stream

text: Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell 405.05 points (-1.76%) to 22,671.86, extending its losing streak as a broad AI and semiconductor selloff weighed on sentiment. Technology stocks led the decline: Tencent -2.28%, AIA -1.94%, SMIC -6.8%, Meituan -2.80%, Xiaomi -3.95%, and Kingboard Laminates -5.2%. Investors remained cautious despite the Hong Kong debut of Apple supplier Lingyi iTech, whose HK$8.3 billion IPO underscored continued interest in AI-related listings but failed to improve broader market sentiment. Financial and producer manufacturing shares also came under pressure.

shanghai_composite.basis: Xinhua: 'down 2.26 percent to 4,027.26 points'; Trading Economics SHANGHAI table Jun/26

chinent.basis: Xinhua: 'lost 4.07 percent to close at 4,194.21 points'

combined_turnover.basis: Xinhua: 'combined turnover of stocks covered by these two indices reached 3.55 trillion yuan (about 521 billion U.S. dollars), down from 3.59 trillion yuan on the previous trading day'

value.sector_leaders: Shares in the education and pork sectors led gains; cobalt metal and battery industry posted sharp declines (Xinhua)

text: Mainland Chinese benchmarks closed sharply lower on Friday. The Shanghai Composite fell 2.26% to 4,027.26, the Shenzhen Component dropped 3.44% to 15,782.22, the ChiNext lost 4.07% to 4,194.21, and the STAR Composite fell 2.02% to 2,343.66. The CSI 300 declined 3.03% to 4,868.22. Combined turnover on the Shanghai and Shenzhen indices reached 3.55 trillion yuan (~$521 billion), down from 3.59 trillion yuan the prior session. Education and pork shares led gains, while cobalt metal and battery industry stocks posted sharp declines — a rotation away from the battery/EV supply chain that mirrored the broader regional tech/chip selloff.

asx_200.basis: Yahoo Finance: 8,764.20 / +0.18%. TE news stream: 'edged up 16 points, or 0.2%, to close at 8,764.' TE CFD stats table: 8,765.80 / +0.20%. Previous close 8,749.90

context.basis: TE ASX news stream: 'the local market fell 0.7% for the week, reversing strength in the prior two sessions'

macro_driver.rba_stance: RBA paused after three hikes since January; Deputy Governor Hauser stressing inflation remains 'too high' (from prior session context). Resilience in recent data stoked concern that the RBA may tighten further.
macro_driver.china_pmis_next_week: Traders cautious ahead of China PMI data and RBA June meeting minutes next week
macro_driver.inflation: Australia CPI eased to 4.00% in May from 4.20%, but core inflation accelerated; RBA rate at 4.35%
macro_driver.gold_lift: Easing fears of an imminent US rate hike lifted bullion, supporting gold producers

gainers.basis: TE ASX200 component table Jun/26 and news stream

banks.note: Three of the big four banks rose modestly (TE news stream)

text: Australia's ASX 200 was the sole regional gainer, edging up 14.30 points (+0.16%) to 8,764.20, as rising copper and gold prices supported miners and process industries, retail trade, and transport stocks lent support. BHP gained 0.8% and Rio Tinto 2.2%. Gold producers Northern Star and Evolution Mining each jumped about 3% as easing fears of an imminent US rate hike lifted bullion. Three of the big four banks rose modestly. However, the market fell 0.7% for the week, reversing strength in the prior two sessions, as resilience in recent data stoked concern that the RBA may tighten further. CSL fell 2.36%. Traders turned cautious ahead of China's PMI data next week and the RBA's June meeting minutes.

usd_jpy.basis: Reuters: 'The yen teetered near its weakest level against the dollar in 40 years at 161.62, beyond the 160 level that many see as a line in the sand for Japanese authorities.'

euro.basis: Reuters: 'The euro was up 0.33% at $1.1407 but was set for a second consecutive weekly loss against the dollar.'

dollar_index.basis: Reuters: 'The dollar index eased but was headed for a second straight weekly gain against peers. The index fell 0.3% to 101.20.'

tokyo_core_cpi.reference: June 2026 (Trading Economics; XTB Morning Wrap: 'Consumer prices in Japan's capital rose 1.6% y/y, ticking up from May's 1.3% and matching consensus forecasts')
tokyo_core_cpi.note: First acceleration in eight months, reinforcing expectations that the BoJ will continue raising interest rates

tokyo_core_core_cpi.basis: XTB Morning Wrap: 'The core-core index—stripping out fresh food and energy—accelerated to 1.9% (vs. 1.6% prior)'
tokyo_core_core_cpi.interpretation: Middle East energy shocks and May's 6.3% wholesale inflation spike are filtering into non-energy items like food

boj_commentary.speaker: BoJ board member Naoki Tamura
boj_commentary.stance: Hawkish — called for rate hikes every few months (Trading Economics news stream)

market_session: Asia session Friday 2026-06-26 (post-close FX quote from NY wrap)
text: The yen teetered near its weakest level in 40 years at 161.62 per dollar, beyond the 160 'line in the sand' for Japanese authorities. The dollar index eased 0.3% to 101.20 but was headed for a second straight weekly gain. The euro rose 0.33% to $1.1407. Tokyo's core CPI accelerated to 1.6% y/y in June from 1.3% in May — the first acceleration in eight months — and the core-core index (excluding fresh food and energy) quickened to 1.9%, reinforcing expectations that the BoJ will continue raising interest rates. BoJ hawk Tamura called for rate hikes every few months. The weak-yen, rising-rate combination adds a complex cross-current for Japanese equities: it supports exporter translations but raises intervention risk and tightens financial conditions.

brent_crude.basis: Reuters: 'Brent crude futures fell 4.24% to $72.07 per barrel.' AP US wrap separately reported Brent down 3.8% to $72.60 (earlier Friday reference). Context: more tankers left the Strait of Hormuz; Saudi Aramco resumed loading at Ras Tanura after nearly four-month halt.

gold.basis: Reuters: 'Spot gold rose 1.5% to $4,086.29 an ounce.' Supportive for Australian gold miners (Northern Star, Evolution Mining each +~3%).

us_10y_yield.basis: Reuters: 'The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes fell 1.75 basis points to 4.375%.'

hormuz_shipping.context: Saudi Aramco resumed oil loading at Ras Tanura terminal after nearly four-month halt (Reuters/LSEG shipping data). However, a cargo vessel (Singapore-flagged Ever Lovely) was hit by a projectile 7.5 nautical miles off Oman's port of Dahit; IMO paused its operation to evacuate over 11,000 stranded seafarers from the Strait of Hormuz (XTB Morning Wrap).
hormuz_shipping.interpretation: Oil prices plunged despite the tanker attack, as the broader supply picture improved with more tankers transiting Hormuz and Aramco resuming loadings. This eased energy-cost pressure on oil-import-dependent Asia (especially Japan, Korea) but weighed on Australian energy names in the prior session.

market_session: NY close cross-asset reference for Asia session Friday 2026-06-26
text: The cross-asset backdrop for the Asia session was mixed. Brent crude plunged 4.24% to $72.07 as more tankers left the Strait of Hormuz and Saudi Aramco resumed loading at Ras Tanura after a nearly four-month halt — even though a cargo ship was struck near Oman and the IMO paused its sailor evacuation operation. Spot gold rose 1.5% to $4,086.29 as easing fears of an imminent US rate hike lifted bullion, a tailwind for Australian gold miners. The US 10-year yield fell 1.75 bp to 4.375%. The dollar index eased 0.3% to 101.20 but was set for a second straight weekly gain. For oil-import-dependent Asia (Japan, Korea), falling oil was a tailwind, but it was overwhelmed by the equity selloff. For Australia, rising gold and copper supported the ASX's outperformance.
